food_subgroup: Braised mixed noodle dish with seafood

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Singapore Hokkien Mee. Nearest parent is a mixed noodle dish with seafood. A new FoodOn concept is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite noodle dishes — braised mixed noodle with prawns (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Singapore Hokkien Mee; A03SM used as closest defensible parent for composite noodle dishes with seafood.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Singapore Hokkien Mee as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre plate of Singapore Hokkien Mee weighs approximately 380 g, as recorded in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (Food Code F05060064).

nutrient_source.title: Hokkien Mee — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F05060064
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo Food Code F05060064 for Hokkien Mee. Core macros and most micronutrients are verified at confidence 1.0. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K are sourced from enriched_json at confidence 0.9; monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data are not reported in the HPB record and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — yellow egg noodles and rice vermicelli mixed; composite GI estimate for Singapore Hokkien Mee
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore Hokkien Mee. GI of 52 is a composite estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for yellow egg noodles and rice vermicelli (bee hoon). The significant protein content from seafood and pork, together with fat from lard and stock, moderates the overall glycemic response below that of plain white noodles. Glycemic load of 32.1 is calculated using 16.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 380 g serving × GI 52 / 100.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — approximately 1805 mg per 380 g serving (78% of the 2300 mg daily recommended limit), driven by prawn stock reduction, soy sauce seasoning, and sambal belacan.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Hokkien Mee to occasional consumption, request reduced sambal and seasoning, and avoid adding extra soy sauce; squeezing calamansi over the dish can enhance flavour without additional sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~52) and a per-serving carbohydrate load of approximately 61.6 g; protein from seafood and pork and fat from lard help attenuate the glycemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Hokkien Mee occasionally as part of a carbohydrate-controlled meal plan, monitoring total carbohydrate intake and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to reduce the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (1.9 g per 100 g; ~7.2 g per serving) from lard and pork belly, combined with high sodium (~1805 mg per serving) and dietary cholesterol (~235 mg per serving), presents a cumulative cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Hokkien Mee to occasional consumption, request preparation without lard, and choose smaller portions to reduce saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Prawns and squid provide modest omega-3 fatty acids; lard contributes saturated fat which may promote inflammation at high intake.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Hokkien Mee is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis when consumed in moderation; requesting preparation without lard reduces the saturated fat content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Singapore Hokkien Mee (Food Code F05060064) is one of Singapore's most iconic hawker dishes, consisting of thick yellow egg noodles and thin rice vermicelli (bee hoon) slow-braised together in a rich, sweet prawn and pork bone stock until the noodles absorb the flavourful broth. The dish is then stir-fried over high heat with whole prawns, squid rings, sliced pork belly, beaten egg, and bean sprouts, giving it a moist, slightly gooey, and deeply savoury character. It is traditionally finished with crispy lard bits for crunch and richness, served alongside a wedge of calamansi lime for acidity and a portion of spicy sambal belacan chilli. Unlike the Klang Valley (KL) Hokkien Mee, which is dark, dry, and soy-sauce-braised, Singapore Hokkien Mee is distinguished by its pale golden, wet, broth-braised preparation. Per 100 g, the dish provides 137 kcal, 6.3 g protein, 5.8 g fat (1.9 g saturated), and 16.2 g carbohydrate, with a high sodium content of 475 mg. A standard hawker-centre serving of 380 g delivers approximately 521 kcal and 1805 mg sodium. The composite glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low), moderated by the protein and fat content. The dish is dairy-free but is not halal due to the use of pork and lard. It is a significant source of selenium (14 mcg per 100 g), vitamin B12 (0.72 mcg), and phosphorus (112 mg), reflecting its seafood and pork content. Clinically, the primary concern is its very high sodium load, making it unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: how many calories in hokkien mee
can a diabetic eat hokkien mee
Singapore hokkien mee vs KL hokkien mee difference

confidence_reason: Core macros and most micronutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (Food Code F05060064) at confidence 1.0. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K sourced from enriched_json at confidence 0.9. GI is an analogous composite estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Overall record confidence is high given the exact HPB match for the primary nutrient profile.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Hokkien Mee (F05060064) is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content is not reported in the HPB record. Copper (0.15 mg), manganese (0.2 mg), and vitamin K (8.5 mcg) are sourced from enriched_json at confidence 0.9 and should be verified against a primary source in a future review cycle. GI of 52 is a composite analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for yellow egg noodles and rice vermicelli; no direct GI measurement for Singapore Hokkien Mee was found in the approved GI source. Glycemic load of 32.1 per serving is calculated as: (16.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 380 g serving / 100) × GI 52 / 100 = 32.1. The dish is not halal due to the use of pork belly and lard. Hindi local name 'होक्कियन मी' is a phonetic transliteration; no established Hindi culinary term exists for this dish.
